title: Explore New Zealand Seismic Data with Infinity and JQ

blocks.content: This dashboard visualizes live earthquake and volcanic activity data from New Zealand's GeoNet monitoring network. You'll see how the Grafana Infinity data source calls a public API, how JQ reshapes the JSON response into tabular data, and how a single API query powers multiple panels through Grafana's dashboard data source.
You'll get your bearings by exploring the two main sections of the dashboard.

The Earthquakes section covers the top half of the dashboard. Stat panels at the top surface the most recent and largest events; below them a full-width geomap plots every earthquake in the dataset, with further charts breaking down activity by magnitude.

Further down, the Volcanoes section shows the current alert level for each of New Zealand's active volcanoes, both on a map and in a colour-coded summary table. Both sections call the GeoNet API directly — no intermediary database needed.
Now that you know the layout, let's dig into the earthquake panels.
You'll explore the key earthquake panels and see how they share a single API query.

This stat panel shows the time of the most recent earthquake. Rather than making its own API call, it references the main earthquake panel as a data source — Grafana's **Dashboard** data source lets any panel consume another panel's query result.
The **Largest Recent** panel finds the biggest earthquake by sorting the same dataset by magnitude before applying the reduce function.

The main geomap is where the data originates. It calls the GeoNet API directly using the Infinity data source and renders two overlapping layers: small marker circles and a heatmap weighted by magnitude, so areas of concentrated seismic activity glow more intensely.
You can see how one API call feeds several panels. Next, let's open the Recent Earthquakes panel to look at the query and JQ expression inside.
You'll open the data hub panel to see the Infinity query, the JQ expression, and how the heatmap layer is configured.
Open the Recent Earthquakes panel for editing.

steps.reftarget: section[data-testid='data-testid Panel header Recent Earthquakes'] svg[data-testid='icon-ellipsis-v']
steps.description: Click here to open the panel menu.
Click Edit to open the panel editor.

blocks.content: The Infinity query calls the GeoNet Earthquake API, fetching all earthquakes with a Modified Mercalli Intensity of 1 or above — the threshold for perceptible shaking. The parser is set to **jq-backend**, so the raw GeoJSON response passes directly through a JQ expression.
Open the parsing options to reveal the JQ expression.

steps.description: Click here to expand the parsing options.

blocks.content: The JQ expression iterates over every item in the GeoJSON features array and projects it into a flat object — extracting the public ID, timestamp, depth, MMI, magnitude, locality, and coordinates. The result is a clean table that all the other earthquake panels consume.
Return to the dashboard to continue.

steps.description: Click here to return to the dashboard.

blocks.content: You've seen how Infinity and JQ turn a raw GeoJSON feed into the table that powers the whole earthquake section. Next, see how transforms reshape that data for the bar chart.
You'll open the bar chart panel and follow the transformation pipeline that groups raw earthquake rows into magnitude bands.
Open the Number of Recent Earthquakes by Magnitude panel for editing.

steps.reftarget: section[data-testid='data-testid Panel header Number of Recent Earthquakes by Magnitude'] svg[data-testid='icon-ellipsis-v']
steps.description: Click here to open the panel menu.

blocks.content: Switch to the Transform data tab.

Five transformations run in sequence to produce the chart:

1. **Calculate field** — applies a floor function to the magnitude, rounding each earthquake down to a whole number band (e.g., 2.7 becomes 2).
2. **Group by** — counts how many earthquakes fall into each band.
3. **Organize fields** — renames magnitude_scale to magnitude and magnitude_scale (count) to occurrences, dropping the raw columns.
4. **Sort by** — orders the bands from lowest to highest magnitude.
5. **Convert field type** — converts the magnitude number to a string so it renders as a categorical x-axis label rather than a numeric axis.
Return to the dashboard to continue.

steps.description: Click here to return to the dashboard.

blocks.content: That transformation chain converts raw rows into a grouped bar chart without touching the underlying query or data source.
You'll open the GeoJSON Feed Example panel to see how the same earthquake data can be displayed as a colour-coded magnitude map.
Open the GeoJSON Feed Example panel for editing.

steps.reftarget: section[data-testid='data-testid Panel header GeoJSON Feed Example'] svg[data-testid='icon-ellipsis-v']
steps.description: Click here to open the panel menu.

blocks.content: The data source here is set to **Dashboard**, pointing at the Recent Earthquakes panel. No second API call is made — this panel simply reuses the table that Infinity + JQ already produced, applying its own colour rules on top.
Return to the dashboard and exit edit mode to wrap up.

steps.description: Click here to return to the dashboard.
Click here to exit edit mode.

blocks.content: You've seen how multiple map panels can share one underlying query to give different perspectives on the same data.
You'll take a quick look at how the volcanoes section follows the same Infinity + JQ pattern as the earthquake panels.

blocks.reftarget: section[data-testid='data-testid Panel header Volcanic Alert Status']
blocks.content: The Volcanic Alert Status map calls a separate GeoNet endpoint using the same Infinity + JQ approach. A JQ expression extracts each volcano's ID, title, location, alert level and colour code. Coloured triangles mark each volcano on the map — green means no elevated activity; yellow, orange and red indicate increasing levels of unrest.

blocks.reftarget: section[data-testid='data-testid Panel header Volcano Alert Summary']
blocks.content: The table alongside lists every monitored volcano with its current alert level, activity description and hazard status. Row colours match the map, making it easy to scan for elevated alerts.
You've now seen the full dashboard — two independent Infinity queries powering an earthquake heatmap, bar chart, and volcanic alert map.

## Further Resources

Here are the tools and data sources behind this dashboard:

- [New Zealand GeoNet API](https://api.geonet.org.nz/) — the free public API providing real-time earthquake and volcano data.
- [Grafana Infinity Data Source](https://grafana.com/grafana/plugins/yesoreyeram-infinity-datasource/) — the plugin that lets Grafana query any HTTP API directly.
- [JQ Playground](https://jqplay.org/) — experiment with JQ expressions interactively in your browser.
- [JQ Manual](https://jqlang.org/manual/) — the full language reference for JQ.
- [UK Carbon Intensity Dashboard](https://play.grafana.org/d/carbon-intensity) — another example using Infinity and JQ to pull live data from a public API.
